title: Pinch pot with sgraffito bird decoration
subtitle: A small air-dry bowl with a scratched bird design through a slip layer
excerpt: A small air-dry pinch pot with a contrasting slip coat and a scratched sgraffito bird design. The black slip provides a dark background; the needle-tool lines reveal the lighter clay body beneath. No kiln required. The finished bowl is approximately 100 mm in diameter and suitable as a decorative dish for jewellery or trinkets.
sourceNotes: Sgraffito through slip is a technique common across European historical pottery traditions. Technique derived from first principles. V&A open-access collection for historical slip-decorated ware reference.

glossaryTerms.definition: A surface decoration technique in which a top layer of slip or paint is scratched away to reveal the body beneath.
Clay diluted with water to a brushable, paint-like consistency.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to carve or scratch.
Fully air-dried and no longer damp. For air-dry clay, this is the structural finish state.

content.text: A pinch pot with a sgraffito decoration combines two skills in one project: forming a small bowl by pinching, and applying
layer to reveal the clay body beneath. No kiln is required. The finished bowl is decorative; it is not food-safe and not waterproof.

items.name: Black acrylic paint or black acrylic slip
items.substitution: Watered-down black acrylic paint works as a slip substitute

content.text: Roll 150 g of clay into a smooth ball. Hold the ball in one palm and press the thumb of your other hand into the centre, stopping 8-10 mm from the base. This creates the opening of the bowl. Pinch the wall outward between thumb and forefinger, rotating the ball a quarter-turn after each pinch. Work from the base upward, aiming for a wall thickness of around 6 mm throughout.
Continue until the bowl is approximately 100 mm wide and 50 mm tall. Flatten the base by pressing the bowl gently on the wooden board. Allow the pot to reach

Mix black acrylic paint with a small amount of water to a thin-cream consistency. This acts as the coloured slip layer. Brush one coat over the exterior of the bowl, covering the surface evenly. Allow to dry for 15-20 minutes until the paint is no longer wet but is still slightly soft.

Sketch the bird design lightly on paper first. A simple profile bird with outstretched wings works well at this scale: a body oval, two wing arcs, a triangular tail, a pointed beak. Use the needle tool to scratch the design through the black paint layer, revealing the lighter clay below. Scratch with firm, decisive strokes. Hesitant lines drag the paint and leave ragged edges.

Add leaf or branch details around the bird to fill the curved surface of the bowl. Fine parallel lines suggest feathers or grass. The contrast between the dark paint and the lighter clay body creates the finished pattern.

, then sand the rim and the base lightly with fine sandpaper to remove rough patches. Apply one coat of clear acrylic varnish over the decorated surface. The varnish seals the paint layer and makes the scratched lines stand out clearly.
Use a white or terracotta paint layer instead of black for a different colour contrast.
Apply the paint to the interior of the bowl instead for a sgraffito interior with a plain exterior.
Build a larger pinch pot (180 mm diameter) and use the extra surface for a more detailed illustration.

rows.problem: Paint tears rather than scratching cleanly
rows.cause: The paint layer was applied too thickly, or the clay beneath is too soft
rows.fix: Allow the paint to dry more before scratching. Apply thinner coats of paint.

rows.cause: Paint layer is too thin, so there is little contrast with the clay body
rows.fix: Apply a second coat of paint and let it dry before scratching.

rows.cause: Walls pinched too thin, or pot dried too fast
rows.fix: Aim for 6 mm wall thickness. Cover with plastic for the first few hours of drying for a more even moisture release.
